topics.title: Radio Industry Consolidation and Listener Tributes
topics.summary: The studio discusses the impact of radio industry mergers, shares stories from listeners regarding family restrictions on media, and pays tribute to a recently deceased songwriter.

topics.title: Tribute to Lonnie Heckman and Carson Daly News
topics.summary: The studio pays tribute to the passing of listener Lonnie Heckman while discussing Carson Daly's decision to produce his late-night show during a writers' strike.

topics.title: Late-Night Talk Show Dynamics and Reality TV
topics.summary: The studio discusses the logistics of late-night talk shows during a writers' strike, upcoming media appearances, and critiques of reality television programs like Dancing with the Stars and The Biggest Loser.

topics.title: Script Evaluation and Film Industry Discussion
topics.summary: The studio explores the merits of a new script, discusses past production experiences with various films, and debates the potential for a modern take on classic monster cinema.

topics.title: Film Pitch and Governor Interview
topics.summary: The studio features a critique of a bizarre horror film pitch involving the resurrection of Boris Karloff followed by an interview with the Governor of California regarding his unconventional proposals for global warming.

topics.title: California Governor's Proposed Initiatives
topics.summary: The show features a call-in from a California official who discusses proposed legislation regarding transgender disclosure, educational incentives for female students, and a satirical proposal to move the Earth away from the Sun.

topics.title: Radio Career History and Industry Conflicts
topics.summary: The studio explores the history of radio broadcasting in New York and Washington, detailing past career decisions, management conflicts over programming styles, and the personal professional journey of the hosts.

topics.title: Colin Quinn Interview
topics.summary: The studio hosts comedian Colin Quinn to discuss his career trajectory, the challenges of stand-up comedy, and personal reflections on past decisions.

topics.title: Colin Quinn's Career and Personal Life
topics.summary: The studio explores comedian Colin Quinn's professional status, his experiences with depression and therapy, his history in the comedy circuit, and his current creative projects.

topics.title: Standby of a 911 Shooting Call
topics.summary: The studio plays a recorded 911 call involving a man who shoots two burglars on neighboring property and discusses the legal implications of his actions.

topics.title: Hostility and Upcoming Show Segments
topics.summary: The studio features a heated exchange regarding a past conflict with another personality, the announcement of an upcoming retrospective series, and a discussion about a planned stunt involving Richard Christie.

topics.title: Ass Bleaching and Studio Conflict
topics.summary: The studio features a discussion regarding the safety of using fruit acid for anal bleaching before transitioning into a heated argument between two staff members over personal remarks and professional respect.

topics.title: Radio Show Segment Transitions
topics.summary: The studio transitions through a series of segments including discussions on personal relationships, celebrity news regarding Joe Rogan and Arnold Schwarzenegger, and a repetitive technical loop during the transition to a new segment.

topics.title: Middle East Diplomacy and NFL Tributes
topics.summary: The studio discusses the complexities of Israeli state identity, the tragic death of Redskins' Sean Taylor, and upcoming comedy tour dates.

transcript.text: "Oh yeah, what a song. Thank you. Yeah, it should have been a big hit, that one. A real heartbreaker. Now I know what it's like for these artists who write these songs. I mean, when you make a beauty like this and it doesn't become a number one song, it's very heartbreaking."
"Why couldn't that be your more human than human?"
"I know."
"I thought it was a bit of a hit now. Did it chart at all?"
"Well, the album was a very big-selling album. Back in the day, you could sell it."
"But no breakout hit, would you think?"
"That was gross."
"That was a rough one."
"Yeah, that sounded like everything came up."

"Did you even hear the fart that accompanied it? Like erupting. Over Thanksgiving, I gained like some weight. Not a lot, you know, for me, but as soon as I go up to like a five pounds, that's when I kick into--"
"You go into diet mode?"
"I go into diet mode. You know, something kicks in and I go, okay, well, I could go up another five pounds or I could just nip this in the bud."
"We haven't figured out where Artie kicks into diet mode."
"No, that's why I don't understand Artie. I don't understand when you don't kick in all of a sudden and go, you know, this is it. Especially the amount of poontang he could be getting."
"With every bad thing, I don't have a kick in."
"I know."
"That's the problem. That's why, I don't know, I'm just, you're a shrink to figure out. I don't know, I'm just an extremist."
"Only you went to a shrink."
"Yeah, the shrink would have to find it out without him ever being there as well."
"And then pass that information on to him in an email. But he doesn't get emails."

"But Thursday, like Thanksgiving night at like midnight, if there's a great movie on and you haven't eaten since three o'clock, the guy who could turn down like the sandwich you were talking about the other day at that point, that's a willpower guy."
"I mean, that's just fun, you know. What are you going to do?"
"Hi, you're on the air. Let's go to John Boy."
"Hey, how's it going? First call of the day. Wow, that's pretty cool. Let me get..."
"Quickly, John Boy, I'm bored already."
"You know, I know, Howard, you're never going to be the type of guy to step on someone's balls and cause them not to make a little extra cash. You're just not that way."
"But when I heard Artie saying he was thinking of putting JD on the payroll, I mean, I got a little concerned right away about JD, you know what I mean?
Do you think this is a good idea, really?"
"Why wouldn't that be a good idea?"

"Oh, a number of reasons, actually. Number one, I don't know if Artie came to you and asked if this would be acceptable because JD already has a job, he has to watch TV, and if he's out running around chasing pussy with Artie."
"I don't know, I'm not too concerned about it."
"It's a weekend. It's one night on a weekend."
"Honestly, it didn't really register with me, but..."
"Rocko, you're on the air."
"John Boy, really thinking."
"I'm thinking a little too hard."
"This is Howard Stern, Rocco. Go ahead from Nicholson, Pennsylvania."
"Hey, dude, what's going on? I just turned 41, and this is the first time I ever heard your show."
"Really?"
"You're kidding. Nicholson, Pennsylvania?"
"Yeah, well, I'm not originally from there, but I was never allowed to listen to your show."
"As a kid, you mean?"
"Up until 41."
"Up until six months ago."
"How old are you?"
"And people were telling you what to do when you were like 30?"
"Well, my father..."
"Listen, when my father said something, you listened."
"Yeah, but, dude, once you hit like 20...20..."
"Oh, bets off!"
"I mean, you can go off and do your own thing, you know, that you get a job and an apartment..."
"You didn't understand who my father was."
"Who was your father?"
"Well, I can't tell you my last name, but it's very similar to the famous person."
"Who's a mafia guy?"
"I wouldn't use that word, but he was in the construction business, you know what I'm saying?"
"Yeah, but, I mean..."
"Well, those guys aren't going to bump off their son for listening to Howard Stern!"
"I'd say they liked Howard, Joe."
"No, he only said he was an asshole and don't listen to him twice."
"So your dad was wrong?"
"Well, what happened recently that you can now listen?"
"My father passed away six months ago and I bought a serious radio and I..."
"On that day?"
"And I said, well, you know, there's nothing going to happen to me now, so let me check it out."
"This cat sounds like he's been living under the Taliban."
"Really?"
"No, no, no, not at all."
"I was 35 when I pierced my ears."
"Well, that's true. You broke out at 35."
"I got tattoos, I got piercings, all that was cool, but..."
"You know, when he told me he was pretty adamant about something, I had to listen..."
"What if you listened and didn't tell him?"
"I'd be afraid he'd find out."
"And what would he do, you think?"
"Cut me off from all..."
"Money?"
"Yeah, family matters."
"Oh, so you live off your dad?"
"No, then you gotta listen to him."
"I don't live off of him, but, you know..."
"He's the head of the family."
"You know, what he said he did, I mean, I remember when I was a kid, taking trips from upstate New York with people in the truck..."
"And then it would end up just being me and him left, and I just wouldn't ask questions, and I grew up that way, you know?"
"Are you like Fredo, like you couldn't take over the family?"
"Yeah, you got a younger brother who's really smart."
"Well, you looked over!"
"Well, you looked over?"
"No, I was taken care of, very good, you know..."
"What do you mean when you looked over, you understand what I'm saying?"
"Oh, yeah, but I didn't fall, it wasn't that serious..."
"Seen the mafia..."
"Who got the family business?"
"My older brother."
"Well, he's the younger brother, this is in the right order."
"I still keep in touch with him, and I still work for him, but not so much anymore, you know?"
"Well, I'm glad you're allowing yourself to listen, it shows that while your father might have been a nice man, or a great man, but he wasn't always right."
"What else was forbidden in your life besides the Howard Stern Show? I imagine there had to be other things."
"Just that?"

"Well, I mean, like I said, no, I just made me think of it, because I listened to your show, and I just, you know, brought back memories of that, I mean, you know, I pretty much grew up kind of normal, other than..."
"Does your name rhyme with body, or balumbo?"
"Uh, about D'Orléans."
"Oh."
"Sure, are you a crazy guy living in the movie?"
"My name was changed, my father changed it after, you know, things kind of..."
"Too much heat from the feds."
"Yeah, he used to work for a construction company in New Jersey, and he helped do the paving of the parking lot of the Meadowlands."
"Oh, so where's Hoffa? Is he there?"
"Well, you know what, with a dad like that, I wouldn't listen to him either."
"Alright, thanks brother, and I'm glad you're listening."
"Oh yes, we're glad to have you now."
"Yeah, last week you had a show, it was about some guy Jackie, all about Jackie, what was that?"
"Oh, we can't update you on 20 years of radio."
"Yeah, you just gotta keep listening, you'll get familiar with stuff, okay?"
"Hey, could I call in sometimes and let you know how things are going?"
"Sure, absolutely Rocco, thank you."
"Okay."
"I see why his dad had to keep it tight."
"Yeah, he couldn't take off."
"I don't know your name, because the computer doesn't allow me to read it, but hello, you're on the air."
"Uh, just Howard?"
"Yes."

"There's BTP, uh, yeah, I heard you say yesterday about Latisha, and I'm a truck driver, and I came back up to run up Jacksonville, and I leave my radio on, regular FM radio for my dogs, while I'm gone."

"And I heard a talk show, lesson Terry, talking about how they're mad at Latisha for going on your show, and riding the Sydney Inn, and just giving her a bunch of shit, like they made her famous, or whatever."
"Who made her famous?"
"Lesson Terry, who was that?"
"Who's lesson Terry?"
"That's what I'm saying, some people out of doubt, they're like really big in Jacksonville."
"I never heard of them, but I can almost guarantee you that the freedom of bringing anybody on the radio, especially a girl, and talking about sex, and talking about uh,

anything on the radio, as a result of this show, and whoever lesson Terry are, oh a huge thanks to this show for anything they're doing right now, I would almost guess, and I don't know, I've never heard their show, but I would guess their show is extremely reminiscent of the style of show that we do."
"When is someone gonna make lesson Terry famous?"
"Yeah, I mean."
"They made her famous how I had never heard of her before."
"I like that lesson Terry are making people famous."
"Yeah, if I'm saying that they're really jealous of you and Bubba and Pharrell, there's no doubt about it."
"Well, all of radio is jealous of us, not just lesson Terry, but the radio industry shows its true colors as they fight this merger, tooth and nail.
They never used to care. I come over to satellite radio, they're completely on the war path over this merger.
This is unbelievable. I read another article today about how anxious Ex-Im and Sirius are to get on with their business and merge, and they cannot get an answer from any officials in our government.
And this is unbelievable when you look at the history of mergers in this country that have been approved by the government.
I told you the war in Iraq didn't take this long to figure out. They spent less time deciding whether to invade a country and remove its dictator than they do on this merger.
There's something wrong. What is going on? And the radio industry is pumping millions and millions and millions of dollars into, I don't know, I don't know where this money goes."
"Yeah, well that's what you have to ask yourself. You're pumping millions, where does it go?"
"And they are spending so much time claiming this is a monopoly, and yet why are they fighting it if it's a monopoly? They obviously compete with us if they are so against it."
"It's obvious. If they didn't truly compete with us, they wouldn't be spending millions of dollars."

"The entire radio industry is jealous of our situation, and it is unbelievable to me that anyone would be upset, especially Les and Terry, with this show. If it wasn't for us, Les and Terry wouldn't have a show.
I don't know Les and I don't know Terry, but I've never heard of the Les and Terry show. But I guarantee you it's horrible."

"Yeah, it is horrible. When I was born and raised, Les and Tom in Jacksonville, I wasn't a big fan of talk radio. I was forced to kind of listen to you because I like music a lot, but I listened to you on some station out of Orlando."
"Well, when we left, I became a fan when I heard you talking about interviewing Willie Nelson, and I was like, 'Man, this guy is really great.' And then you said you were going to add that."
"When I think back on my time in terrestrial radio, some 20-something, 30-something years, I don't even know how many years."
"Yeah, I don't remember."

"I don't chart that crap. Some guys, they know every anniversary. I think back, and now we left, and they can't even find anyone who can come close to accomplishing what our show accomplished, which is to maintain ratings, and revenue, and maintain an audience in the morning."
"Across the country."
"They can't even find it in one market. Forget about the country. They gave it up on that."
"They thought it would be easy."
"You're already a genius. You're a programming genius. I think you're one of the greatest, and I just want to say, Artie, Donnie, put my foot in your ass, Artie."
"All right, thank you."
"What did he say, Art?"
"It's a catchphrase from Mr. Catchphrase."
"Anyway, I love y'all. I'll get off the air, man. I appreciate talking to y'all."
"All right, thank you. I didn't even know you had a catchphrase."
"That's a mad TV."
"Did you coin that?"
"I had a full-blown catchphrase."
"What is your catchphrase?"
"I did a character called, I told you about this before, called White Mama. I played a black mother in an offensive sitcom that we used to die. I did four sketches.
And it was supposed to be a bad WB sitcom, and my catchphrase was, 'Don't make me break my foot off in your ass.'"
"I would scream it at my daughter, who was the black girl on the show and into the camera, and the kids for a long time, like 13-year-olds would watch the show and say that to me."
"That's kind of so, like every minute, Mama would go, 'Don't make me break my foot off in your ass.'"
"I'll bring it in one day for you to watch it. The sketch is--"
"Did you do it in blackface?"
"No. Well, what the sketch is is, we were trying to figure out what would, like, the WB had the Wayans brothers on at the time."
"Right."
"We were thinking, like, what do these white TV executives think a black show is?"
"So, we thought of what the most offensive sitcom would be, and we thought of--"
"It's me driving down the street, eating a cheeseburger, and I hit a black woman and kill her."
"And I go out to see if she's all right, and her soul enters my body, and I become her."
"Oh, good."
"I take over her life."
"So, it's no blackface. It's just me and, like, a curler and a moo-moo, and I just look like me."
"And the black girl on the show, who's brilliant, is Deborah Wilson. She would play my daughter, and the whole show is her trying to make out with boyfriends, and I'm screaming at her."
"Don't make me break my foot off in your ass!"
"Don't make me break my foot off in your ass!"
"So, that was a recurring role I did."
"Hey, I have sad news for everyone this morning, and I just got wind of this."
"Really?"
"I was reading my email this morning that Fred forwarded to me."
"Uh, is somebody dead?"
"Yeah, unfortunately, yes, and this is really sad, actually, and I feel awful."
"Awful, okay."
"Let me prepare my tribute, if you would give me one second."
"That is actually Fred playing the trumpet."
"Fred practiced all night?"
"No, that's him. If you look over, he has a trumpet in his mouth."
"Yeah, that's the thing. He doesn't usually play the trumpet, so he wants to practice."
"Good for you, Fred."
"Beautiful."
"I'm a little revelry, that, huh?"
*laughter*
"What's revelry?"
"Yeah."
"He was a boogie, we'll give you a good boy from Company B."
*laughter*
"Alright, this is a letter that actually J.D. forwarded to me."
"I don't know if you guys heard, but Lonnie Heckman died this morning."
"Wait a minute, hold it."
"But you're going to say 'hand over,' I really got scared."
"No."
"Who's Lonnie Heckman?"
"You know."
"Our reporter, Dustin Schufe, was working on a story about Lonnie. The article was a preview of Shaw Blade's
appearance Thursday at Crocodile Rock, the Killers of Comedy. Lonnie was the guy who wrote 'When I See Beth Smiling,'
who we interviewed two weeks ago."
"He said he had some medical problems."
"Well, he had, you know, I kind of blew by that, but Lonnie said he had medical problems. I didn't know they were that serious."
"Yeah."
"Lonnie wrote, here's Lonnie's version. He sent this in the mail, and remember I was goofing on it saying, 'I don't think it's a very good song.'
Meanwhile, it turned out to be a good song."
"And we've talked to him in everything."
"Yeah, so here's Lonnie, if you don't know this yet, he wrote this song. Sent it to us in the mail, and, you know,
we goofed on it and stuff, and ended up calling Lonnie, and he turned out to be a real nice guy."
"When I see Beth smiling, time just fades away."
"Well, now the song gets to me."
"It's poignant now."
"No, he died."
"You got to die in music."
"So Lonnie was excited because Shaw Blades has been, they've been doing concerts all over the country singing 'When I See Beth Smiles.'
So they were doing a story on him in his local newspaper, how he wrote this song, and he's getting some notoriety,
and Shaw Blades, Tommy Shaw, was, you know, singing this.
The guy writes in his email, 'As you can imagine, the story has changed. Is there anyone at the show that Dustin may be able to speak to
about Lonnie's passing?' Yeah, have him call in.
"Wow."
"We're writing a story for tomorrow's paper. We would like to speak to someone today. Give us a call."
"So how did, what killed him?"
"You know, I don't, that's why I wanted to get this guy on this reporter. He seems to know more about it than we do, but,
here's Shaw Blades' version.
And Lonnie, don't know what happened yet, but really sorry about this, and very sad."
"Yeah, you got to know him."
"Yeah. Lonnie, I think was going to write a follow-up, too."
"And you remember that the Bleeding Deacons even did a version of it?"
"Which inspired even more people to send in songs."
"I mean, a lot of people got inspired."
"That's a band called Blue Collar, so everyone got inspired by Lonnie."
"I became a highly covered song."
"Now, how do you know this Fred and Benji, I'm not sure who's, is it, Benji, what did he die from?"
"Reportedly on the Lehigh Valley News, from Kidney Failure Complications."
"Lisa G's coming in apparently, they've been all over the store and have some more information."
"Okay, so Lonnie Heckman, who was just on our show, I think a week or two ago."
"Yeah, he died from Kidney Failure Complications."
"How old a guy was Lonnie?"
"Wow, and was he suffering for a long time with this kidney thing?"
"He had these complications from diabetes."
"Was he a heavy guy?"
"No."
"Was he like a lifelong sufferer of diabetes, or was it an adult onset?"
"Gosh, I don't remember, but he had two heart attacks."
"Oh my God."
"Kidney failure, he needed a kidney transplant, a pancreatic transplant."
"Wow."
"He was very, very ill, and I mean, talking to him, you would never know."
"You would never know in it, yeah."
"Boy.
Well, look, what can I say, Lonnie, and to your family, sorry to hear about all this."
"Yeah, we didn't even know what his family situation was."
"No."
"He really told us he was on disability, and that's when we found out that there was some kind of..."
"That's when we got jealous."
"Yeah."
"We were like, 'Why, how do you get to sit home and write songs and be disabled?'"
"Because he sounded like there was really nothing wrong."
"I spoke to Tommy Shaw last night, and they have a statement.
Do you want me to read it?"
"Yeah.
Tommy Shaw of Shaw Blades."
"Yes.
The band says, quote, 'We were saddened to hear of the passing of Lonnie Heckman today,
though we never met.
The power of music drew us together to create something bigger than all of us.
Our sympathies go out to his friends and family.
As the righteous brothers once sang, 'If there's a rock and roll heaven,
you know they've got a hell of a band.
Well, now they have a new songwriter, 'Shine On Brother.'"
"Wow, that's beautiful."
"Wow."
"Wow, that was nice."
"Now, they know what to say when somebody dies.
Look at us fumpering around."
"Well..."
"Give me that, and I'm going to say..."
"Well, that's the guy who wrote, 'I look to the sea.'"
"All I know is I'm sitting here and I'm going, 'Well, brother..."
"You didn't even say brother."
"I didn't even say that.
I suck.
I hope I never have to give a eulogy.
My biggest fear is my parents are never going to die,
but if they ever did and I had to give a eulogy,
I would be so bad at it.
No one would cry.
I'm horrible with that kind of stuff.
I mean, I really feel sad about Lonnie's passing."
"Much like a comedian counting laughs,
you would be counting the cries of the eulogy?"
"Yeah."
"Well, today..."
"I really would.
I see other people, like when Dr. Liu's dad died,
I don't go to a lot of funerals.
I'm not invited."
"Do you think people don't think you're good for people?"
"No, you're too tall.
They can't see the pulpit."
"No, Dr. Liu got up and said words.
And I think I cried."
"You talked about it.
You moved and you produced tears."
"He's a doctor.
I should be a great orator.
And then I went to Jerry Nackman, his memorial service."
"I remember you talking about that, yeah."
"His memorial service."
"His memorial service.
And what's that guy's name got up?"
"Jerry Delafimina, yeah."
"Jerry Delafimina.
He got up and made like a eulogy of speech.
Guy was frigging brilliant.
I was like, 'Wow!'
Listen to this guy.
He's relaxed.
That's how I should be."
"I gave my father's eulogy.
That was rough, man."
"Yeah, but you're a good public speaker."
"And did you get tears?"
"I did not.
I told the story, I was only 22,
so not a lot of people knew about the story
about when we were at the World Series
and he threw me on the field.
And God."
"What a tribute."
"No, but it was just, you know."
"I planned to talk about how my father
used to yell at me at his recording stage."
"It was just like, you know,
it's something that people who knew my father
was a typical thing that, you know."
"Did you think you should have
opened with the pig coke story?"
"It didn't happen yet."
"Oh, okay."
"You hadn't reached that height."
"But, I don't know, because I'm on Senior,
it was like, you know, you don't have to do this.
I was like, 'No, I really want to do this.'"
"Did you cry through it and stuff?"
"At the end I got choked up, but it went,
it did go, it went good.
To the point where I wish I had it on tape
because I could live it forever, maybe."
"I'm thinking of actually using
the Delafemina eulogy for anybody who does."
"They just had a different name."
"Really? I mean, it would work with anyone?"
"Talk about what a great newspaper man my father was."
"I've done it twice, and it sucks."
"Eulogizing somebody."
"How are you at it? Do people cry?"
"Well, I'll tell you what happened.
The first time I did it was with my brother,
but I was just so shell-shocked
that I didn't think I did anything good."
"Your father, you're a good person.
I ended up making everything about me
and how I feel."
"See, I did it for my father, it was really good.
You know what I mean? It was really good.
But what I did for my brother, it was terrible."
"Come in and do your father's eulogy.
I want to hear it."
"I haven't ever written down, actually."
"Go read it to us."
"I've got to see if it's still on the computer."
"Maybe I'll borrow some of it."
"But the thing that sucked with the one for my brother,
I knew it sucked, you know what I mean?
Like somebody had to get up,
and we were just all so fucking sad
and I knew it sucked, and then the whole family
tells you how good it was, but you know it's not.
Because when they see it, they have to say something.
And they're like, "Good job."
But nobody could point out anything
in particular that was good about it,
because it wasn't any good.
"I'm a weird guy.
Like I'll get up and give a shitty eulogy,
and then like a day later I'll think of a really good one."
"Right."
"You know, I'm always a day late."
"I shouldn't have said this."
"Let me try again with Lonnie, okay?
Let me see if I can do something nice here."
"It hasn't been a day."
"Friends."
"No, let me hold it.
Let me get it."
"That's good."
"You say you're not a good public figure,
but I've seen that you are."
"We don't know how he is with eulogy."
"All right, let me hold up."
"Oh, I see him, though.
We got pretty good, you know."
"Ladies and gentlemen."
"Yeah."
"Let me start with that."
"Ladies and gentlemen, let me say a little bit of Lonnie."
"Let me, let me, seriously, let me come from the heart."
"All right.
Do you, can you find?"
"No.
There's barely a beat in there,
but I want to say something about a fan of our show
and someone who contributed to our show
and talk about this little radio family we have.
You know, every once in a while, we get on the radio
and we play material from the audience.
We don't do it often,
but there's a connection made
because this radio show is such a big family, in a way,
that we all get together.
So when somebody sends us material and we respond to it,
you feel like, you know, the person.
Now, I never met Lonnie Heckman.
I did interview him on the radio,
and you can sense when somebody is a good soul
and when you get this piece of material,
everyone knows the song, "When You See Beth Smiling."
I was really moved.
I was really touched because here's a guy who cared so much about me
that he actually bothered to write a wedding song
and respond to something we said on the radio.
And the few minutes I shared with him on the phone,
he was one of those guys that didn't even bring up the fact, really,
that he was that sick.
We didn't know because he was one of those guys
who was probably really suffering with a lot of pain,
and he didn't want to burden us with his pain.
Oh, that's a nice thing.
Which is so unlike you, yes.
Yeah, that's... I mean, really, it is a...
You're ruining my religion.
Is Robin going to be up there with you?
Lisa G. is tearing up.
Lisa, am I getting to you?
Yeah, that's good.
Oh, whoa.
That's good.
I'd like to hear what Beth doesn't know, does she?
